The study of mitochondrial dynamics reveals a complex interplay between fusion and fission events, which are critical for maintaining cellular homeostasis. The protein Mitofusin-2 (MFN2) plays a pivotal role in outer mitochondrial membrane fusion. Conversely, Dynamin-related protein 1 (DRP1) is recruited to the mitochondrial surface to mediate fission. Dysregulation of this balance has been implicated in various neurodegenerative diseases, including Parkinson's and Alzheimer's.

Our research focuses on the post-translational modifications of DRP1 and their impact on its enzymatic activity. We hypothesize that phosphorylation at Serine 616 enhances DRP1 recruitment and subsequent mitochondrial fragmentation, leading to increased oxidative stress. This process could represent a key pathological mechanism.
